one_line_takeaway: INSEC shows that a single short, cheaply-optimized comment injected into completion queries can push production black-box code assistants (including GPT-3.5-Turbo-Instruct and GitHub Copilot) to emit insecure code ~50% more often with almost no loss of functional correctness.
key_finding: INSEC raises the insecure-generation rate by roughly 50% absolute (up to ~60%) across all seven engines while keeping functional correctness largely intact (under 22% relative passRatio@1 drop). Strikingly, the two commercial black-box services are attacked with virtually no functional-correctness loss (GPT-3.5-Turbo-Instruct passRatio@1 = 101%, Copilot = 96%), and stronger/larger models retain MORE correctness under attack, suggesting more capable future models may be more, not less, exposed. The attack is cheap (~$5.80 for one CWE on GPT-3.5; ~$6-12 / ~6 GPU-hours for open models; under $10 total), composable across up to 16 CWEs simultaneously, and transfers across models.
core_contribution: The paper introduces INSEC, presented as the first black-box adversarial attack against LLM-based code-completion engines that stealthily biases them toward generating insecure code while preserving functional correctness. INSEC's own contribution (distinct from the cited white-box poisoning line of work) is a query-only attack: it inserts a short, precomputed adversarial string as a code comment on the line directly above the completion location, modifying only the prefix and leaving the suffix intact. The string is found by a black-box, query-based random optimization algorithm (mutate token candidates, keep top-n by measured vulnerability rate) seeded from a set of hand-designed initialization schemes. Because the string is fixed at inference time and inserted indiscriminately into every query, deployment overhead is negligible. The authors also demonstrate a concrete deployment: a benign-looking VS Code plug-in that injects INSEC into the installed GitHub Copilot extension.
threat_model: A realistic black-box setting (their explicit framing, Figure 1 / Section 3.1). A user interacts with a code editor served by a remote black-box completion engine G. The attacker cannot touch model internals (no access to weights, training data, logits, or the target's tokenizer) and cannot retrain/host their own model. The attacker instead controls G's INPUT, defining an adversarial transform f_adv so that G_adv(q) = G(f_adv(q)). Attacker goal: maximize the rate of vulnerable completions in security-critical contexts while satisfying two stealth constraints — (ii) preserve functional correctness (passRatio) and (iii) keep latency/compute overhead low — so unassuming users keep trusting and adopting the suggestions. Assumed delivery vectors include malicious IDE plug-ins (demonstrated), request interception, supply-chain compromise, or a malicious API wrapper. This is explicitly contrasted with (a) prior white-box weight/data poisoning attacks and (b) jailbreak attacks where the user IS the attacker; INSEC's novelty is the stealth + black-box + functional-correctness-preserving combination.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) INSEC still incurs some functional-correctness loss on weaker engines (e.g., StarCoder-3B passRatio@1 = 78%, StarCoder2-3B = 84%); a future objective could explicitly preserve correctness. (2) Optimization/evaluation is per single CWE by default; the multi-CWE result, while promising, degrades functional correctness as more strings are concatenated (aligned with the attack-length ablation). (3) The vulnerability judge is CodeQL, a static analyzer prone to false positives in general — mitigated here by CWE-specific queries and manual validation showing 98% accuracy on the test set, but still a measurement dependency. (4) For ethical reasons the authors do NOT perform end-to-end real-world deployment and withhold concrete optimized attack strings (only dummy strings shown), so real-world efficacy is inferred, not directly measured. (5) Scope is function/repository-level completion; extension to coding agents is left as future work. Reviewer note: the empirical comparison is G vs G_adv plus internal ablations — prior white-box poisoning attacks and the concurrent RAG-injection work of Yang et al. are discussed as related work, not reproduced as baselines.

datasets_used: D_vul: authors' custom vulnerability dataset of 16 CWEs across 5 programming languages, 12 security-critical completion tasks per CWE, split evenly into train/val/test
HumanEval-based multilingual functional-correctness dataset (built on MultiPL-E / HumanEval, GPT-4-generated reference solutions), ~140 validation and ~600 test completion tasks per language
HumanEval-X (used to validate that GPT-4-generated reference solutions match human-written ones)
RepoBench: 333 randomly chosen repository-level completion instances (for EM/ES/CodeBleu similarity)
quantitative_results: Overall: up to ~60% absolute increase in vulnerability rate; under 22% relative decrease in passRatio@1
Cost: ~$5.80 to develop the attack for one CWE on GPT-3.5-Turbo-Instruct (2.1M input + 1.3M output tokens); ~$6-12 and ~6 GPU-hours for open-weight models; under $10 total
Cross-model transfer to GPT-3.5-Turbo-Instruct: strings optimized on StarCoder-3B and CodeLlama-7B raise its vulRate from 22% to 55% and 59% respectively (vs 73% for a directly-optimized string)
Multi-CWE composition: even with all 16 CWEs targeted simultaneously, vulRate is ~2x the unattacked engine
Comment vs no-comment ablation: comment formatting gives +6% vulnerability rate and +11% functional correctness
Chosen hyperparameters: attack-string length 5 tokens for StarCoder-3B (10 for some stronger models); candidate pool size n = 20
CodeQL judge manually validated at 98% accuracy on the test set
RepoBench similarity (ratio of attacked to clean): scores degrade by at most 16.2%, on average 8.1% across EM/ES/CodeBleu
baselines_compared: Unattacked engine G (vulRate and passRatio) vs INSEC-attacked G_adv — the primary comparison, run for all 7 engines
Ablation: Initialization-only vs Optimization-only vs Initialization+Optimization (Init alone reaches ~50% vulRate; full pipeline substantially higher)
Ablation: 7 attack insertion positions (line above chosen as best tradeoff)
Ablation: proxy tokenizer choice — Unicode-char, GPT-2, CodeQwen (used), and the target StarCoder tokenizer
Ablation: attack-string length (1..160 tokens) and candidate pool size n (1..160)

Prior white-box poisoning attacks (Schuster et al., He & Vechev, Aghakhani/TrojanPuzzle, Yan et al.) and concurrent RAG-injection work (Yang et al.) are discussed as related work / context, NOT reproduced as empirical baselines
